facts: Thermal energy is the total kinetic energy of all particles in an object; a larger object at the same temperature has more thermal energy because it contains more particles.
Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles, not the total; a small hot pan can have a higher temperature than a large warm pool but less thermal energy.
Heat is the transfer of thermal energy from a hotter object to a cooler object, not a substance that flows into or out of an object.
Conduction transfers thermal energy through direct contact between particles; metals conduct better than wood because their particles are more closely packed and free electrons help move energy.
Convection transfers thermal energy by the movement of fluids: warmer, less dense fluid rises while cooler, denser fluid sinks, creating a current that redistributes energy.
Radiation transfers thermal energy through electromagnetic waves without needing a medium; the Sun's energy reaches Earth through radiation, not by conduction or convection.
misconceptions: Students think heat and temperature are the same, so they believe a large bucket of warm water has the same amount of heat as a small cup of hot water if they feel equally warm.
Students believe cold is a substance that moves from an ice cube into their hand, making the hand cold, rather than understanding that heat transfers from the hand to the ice.
Students think that in convection, heat itself rises (e.g., 'heat rises from a radiator'), when actually the warmer fluid rises because it is less dense, carrying thermal energy with it.
